questions.stem: Which company may legitimately present financial statements WITHOUT a cash flow statement?

options.text: No company — the cash flow statement is universal
options.explanation: The definition expressly lets OPCs, small companies and dormant companies drop it.

options.text: A One Person Company, small company or dormant company
options.explanation: The s.2(40) definition itself carves these out — they may omit the cash flow statement.

options.text: Any private company, regardless of size
options.explanation: Private status alone earns no exemption — the carve-out is for OPCs, small companies and dormant companies.
Listing is irrelevant to the s.2(40) exemption.

readLink.label: Read: The statutory frame →

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.2(40) proviso

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Books of account
questions.stem: Books of account of a company must ordinarily be preserved in good order for a minimum of:

options.text: Eight financial years immediately preceding the current year
options.explanation: s.128(5) — eight years (or all years, if the company is younger), longer where an investigation directs.
Ten years overshoots the s.128(5) minimum of eight.
Five years is not the statutory floor — s.128(5) fixes eight.
Three years is far short of the eight-year minimum.

readLink.label: Read: The statutory frame →

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.128(5)

questions.stem: For an asset to be classified as CURRENT under Schedule III, it must satisfy:

options.explanation: Requiring all four would exclude almost everything — the tests are alternatives, not cumulative conditions.

options.text: ANY ONE of the tests — realisation in the operating cycle, held for trading, realisation within twelve months, or being cash/a cash equivalent
options.explanation: The four criteria are alternatives; meeting any single one makes the asset current.
The operating-cycle, trading and cash tests are equally sufficient routes to current classification.

options.text: Both the twelve-month test and the operating-cycle test
options.explanation: Either test alone suffices — that is exactly how a 15-month-cycle company treats 14-month inventory as current.

readLink.label: Read: The classification tests →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — general instructions, current assets

questions.stem: A manufacturer holds raw material for 2 months, processes it for 1 month, holds finished goods for 2 months and collects receivables in 3 months. Its operating cycle is:

options.explanation: 3 months is just the collection leg; the cycle spans the whole inventory-to-cash journey.
5 months stops at finished goods and ignores the 3-month collection period — the cycle runs to CASH.
Twelve months is only the DEFAULT when the cycle cannot be identified — here it can be, at 8 months.
Operating cycle = acquisition to cash realisation: 2 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 8 months.

readLink.label: Read: The Schedule III balance sheet →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — operating cycle

questions.stem: Where a company's normal operating cycle cannot be identified, Schedule III directs that it be assumed to be:

options.explanation: The default is twelve months, not two years.
Schedule III prescribes a flat twelve-month assumption, not an industry benchmark.
No six-month default exists in Schedule III.
The Schedule's default: an unidentifiable cycle is taken as twelve months.

readLink.label: Read: The Schedule III balance sheet →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — operating cycle default

questions.stem: A ₹10,00,000 term loan is repayable in five equal annual instalments, the first falling due four months after the balance sheet date. The amount shown under CURRENT liabilities is:

options.explanation: The whole loan is not current — only the slice due within twelve months of the reporting date.
Only the instalment due within twelve months (10,00,000 ÷ 5 = ₹2,00,000) is a current maturity; ₹8,00,000 stays under long-term borrowings.
An instalment due in four months is squarely within twelve months — it must move to current liabilities.
₹4,00,000 counts two instalments, but only one falls due within the next twelve months on these dates.

readLink.label: Read: The classification tests →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — current maturities of long-term debt

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Share application money
questions.stem: Share application money pending allotment, NOT due for refund, appears in the Schedule III balance sheet:

options.text: Inside share capital under shareholders' funds
options.explanation: Until allotment there are no shares — it cannot sit inside share capital.
Application money is not a borrowing — it awaits conversion into equity.

options.text: Under other current liabilities in every case
options.explanation: Other current liabilities is the home only for amounts DUE FOR REFUND (or where allotment is overdue).

options.text: On its own line between shareholders' funds and non-current liabilities
options.explanation: The Schedule gives it a dedicated position — it is neither equity yet nor a borrowing.

readLink.label: Read: The Schedule III balance sheet →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — share application money pending allotment

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Current liability test
questions.stem: A liability must be classified as CURRENT where the company:

options.text: Has no unconditional right to defer its settlement beyond twelve months from the reporting date
options.explanation: That is the fourth Schedule III test — a conditional or absent deferral right forces current classification.

options.text: Intends to refinance the liability after the year end
options.explanation: Intention to refinance does not create a present unconditional right to defer.

options.text: Classifies the matching asset as non-current
options.explanation: Asset and liability classifications are tested independently — there is no matching rule.

options.text: Merely expects, based on past practice, that the lender will roll the debt over
options.explanation: Expectation is not an UNCONDITIONAL RIGHT — without the right, the liability is current.

readLink.label: Read: The classification tests →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — current liabilities criteria

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Share capital note
questions.stem: Vindhya Foods began the year with 5,00,000 equity shares and issued bonus shares in the ratio 1:5 during the year. The share-reconciliation note shows closing shares outstanding of:

options.explanation: 5,20,000 divides by 25; one-fifth of 5,00,000 is 1,00,000 new shares.
5,00,000 ignores the bonus issue — the note must show the movement to 6,00,000.
10,00,000 doubles the capital — a 1:5 bonus adds only one share per five held.

readLink.label: Read: The disclosures examiners test →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — reconciliation of shares outstanding

questions.stem: The share capital note must separately disclose shareholders holding:

options.text: More than 5% of the shares, with the number of shares held
options.explanation: Schedule III requires naming each more-than-5% holder and the shares they hold.
The Schedule III threshold is 5%, not 10%.
1% is below the disclosure line — the requirement bites above 5%.

options.text: Any number of shares, if they are directors
options.explanation: Directors' holdings have their own homes elsewhere; this note is driven purely by the 5% threshold.

readLink.label: Read: The disclosures examiners test →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — shareholders holding more than 5%

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Revenue from operations
questions.stem: Betwa Ceramics billed customers ₹59,00,000 during the year, which includes GST of ₹9,00,000 collected for the government. Revenue from operations in the statement of profit and loss is:

options.explanation: ₹45,00,000 deducts too much — only the ₹9,00,000 GST comes off the billing.
₹68,00,000 ADDS the GST again instead of stripping it out.
₹59,00,000 wrongly treats the GST collected as the company's own revenue.
GST is collected on behalf of government and is not the company's revenue: 59,00,000 − 9,00,000 = ₹50,00,000.

readLink.label: Read: The statement of profit and loss →

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 11 U1 — revenue from operations excludes taxes collected

questions.stem: The Division I statement of profit and loss classifies expenses:

options.text: By whichever basis the directors consider more informative
options.explanation: The format is prescribed, not elective.

options.text: By FUNCTION — cost of sales, administrative, selling and distribution
options.explanation: Function-wise classification is not the Division I format.
Cash/non-cash is a cash-flow-statement idea, not the P&L classification axis.

options.text: By NATURE — materials consumed, employee benefits, finance costs, depreciation, other expenses
options.explanation: Division I prescribes nature-wise heads; there is no cost-of-sales/admin/selling split.

readLink.label: Read: The statement of profit and loss →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — Part II statement of P&L

questions.stem: A company with total income of ₹250 crore must round the figures in its financial statements to the nearest:

options.explanation: Rounding is MANDATORY under Division I as amended, not optional.
No tens-of-crores band exists in the Schedule.
Hundreds/thousands belong to the below-₹100-crore band.

options.text: Lakhs, millions or crores, or decimals thereof
options.explanation: Total income of ₹100 crore or more → rounding to lakhs, millions or crores (or decimals) is the prescribed band.

readLink.label: Read: The disclosures examiners test →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — general instructions, rounding off

questions.stem: Contingent liabilities (claims not acknowledged as debts, guarantees given) are presented:

options.text: In the notes to accounts — never on the face of the balance sheet
options.explanation: Schedule III places contingent liabilities and commitments in the notes.

options.text: As a deduction from reserves and surplus
options.explanation: Nothing is deducted from reserves for a contingency that is merely disclosed.

options.text: On the face of the balance sheet under current liabilities
options.explanation: A contingent item is not a recognised liability — it stays off the face.
The directors' report is no substitute for the notes-to-accounts disclosure.

readLink.label: Read: The disclosures examiners test →

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — contingent liabilities and commitments

questions.stem: At the year end a company holds: treasury bills bought two months before maturity ₹3,00,000; a six-month fixed deposit ₹5,00,000; listed equity shares ₹2,00,000; cash on hand ₹50,000. Cash and cash equivalents for AS 3 total:

options.explanation: ₹8,50,000 wrongly admits the six-month fixed deposit — the three-month test is applied at ACQUISITION.
₹10,50,000 sweeps in the equity shares too — their value risk disqualifies them.
T-bills within three months of acquisition ₹3,00,000 + cash ₹50,000. The 6-month FD fails the acquisition-term test; equity shares carry value risk.
₹50,000 forgets the two-month treasury bills, a textbook cash equivalent.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — cash and cash equivalents

questions.stem: The three-month yardstick for a cash equivalent is measured from:

options.text: The date the investment is sold
options.explanation: Disposal dates are irrelevant to the classification.
Measuring from the reporting date would convert every maturing deposit into a cash equivalent — the test is at acquisition.

options.text: The date of ACQUISITION of the investment
options.explanation: AS 3 tests the term at acquisition — a 6-month deposit never becomes a cash equivalent merely by ageing.

options.text: The start of the financial year
options.explanation: The financial year has nothing to do with the instrument's term.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — short maturity from date of acquisition

questions.stem: Net profit ₹4,20,000; depreciation ₹80,000; loss on sale of machinery ₹10,000; debtors increased ₹30,000; inventory decreased ₹20,000; creditors increased ₹15,000. Cash generated from operations (before tax) is:

options.explanation: ₹5,00,000 misses the creditors increase of ₹15,000, which releases cash.
₹5,45,000 adds the debtors increase; growing debtors LOCK UP cash and are deducted.
₹4,95,000 subtracts the ₹10,000 loss on sale — a loss is a NON-CASH debit and must be added back.

readLink.label: Read: Building the statement →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — indirect method

questions.stem: For a manufacturing company, interest PAID on a term loan and interest RECEIVED on investments are classified respectively as:

options.explanation: This swaps the two rules — paid goes to financing, received to investing.
Non-financial enterprise: servicing borrowings is financing, returns on investments are investing.
Interest received is a return ON investment — investing, not financing.
Operating treatment of interest belongs to FINANCIAL enterprises, not manufacturers.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — interest and dividends, non-financial enterprise

questions.stem: For a BANK, interest paid on deposits and interest earned on advances are classified as:

options.explanation: That split is the NON-financial-enterprise rule; a bank's interest flows are its operations.
A bank's core interest flows are operating, not investing.

options.text: Excluded from the cash flow statement
options.explanation: They are cash flows like any other — classified under operating for a bank.
For a financial enterprise, lending and borrowing ARE the principal revenue-producing activity, so both flows are operating.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — financial enterprises

questions.stem: A non-financial company: purchased machinery ₹6,00,000; sold old machinery for ₹1,50,000; purchased investments ₹2,00,000; received dividend ₹40,000. Net cash flow from INVESTING activities is:

options.explanation: (₹8,10,000) treats the machinery sale proceeds as an outflow instead of an inflow.
(₹6,50,000) leaves out the ₹40,000 dividend received, an investing inflow here.
−6,00,000 + 1,50,000 − 2,00,000 + 40,000 = −₹6,10,000 — dividend received is investing for a non-financial company.
(₹4,10,000) omits the investment purchase of ₹2,00,000.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — investing activities

questions.stem: During the year a company issued equity shares for ₹8,00,000, repaid a term loan ₹3,00,000, paid dividend ₹1,20,000 and paid interest ₹60,000. Net cash flow from FINANCING activities is:

options.explanation: ₹3,80,000 omits the interest paid, a financing outflow for a non-financial company.
₹5,00,000 nets only the share issue and loan repayment, dropping both distribution outflows.
₹2,60,000 deducts the interest twice.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — financing activities

questions.stem: Under AS 3, cash flows arising from taxes on income are classified as:

options.text: Excluded, since tax is an appropriation of profit
options.explanation: Tax paid is a real cash outflow and always appears in the statement.
Taxes are not financing flows by default.

options.text: Operating activities, unless specifically identifiable with financing or investing transactions
options.explanation: The default is operating; only a tax traceable to a specific investing/financing transaction follows that activity.
Only a tax specifically attributable to an investing transaction goes there.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — taxes on income

questions.stem: Provision for tax: opening balance ₹90,000, closing balance ₹1,05,000; the charge to P&L for the year was ₹1,20,000. Tax actually PAID during the year is:

options.explanation: ₹90,000 is just the opening provision, not the payment.
₹1,20,000 is the P&L CHARGE; the cash flow statement needs the amount actually paid.
Paid = opening 90,000 + charge 1,20,000 − closing 1,05,000 = ₹1,05,000.
₹1,35,000 adds the closing balance instead of deducting it.

readLink.label: Read: Building the statement →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — taxes paid; provision account reconstruction

questions.stem: Equity shares issued to a vendor as consideration for machinery acquired are:

options.text: Shown as a financing inflow and an investing outflow of equal amount
options.explanation: Grossing up a cashless deal fabricates flows that never happened.

options.text: Excluded from the cash flow statement and disclosed separately
options.explanation: No cash moved — AS 3 keeps non-cash investing/financing transactions out of the statement, with separate disclosure.
It is neither operating nor a cash flow.

options.text: Shown only as an investing outflow
options.explanation: There was no outflow of cash at all.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — non-cash transactions

questions.stem: Machinery account: opening balance ₹8,00,000, closing balance ₹9,50,000. Depreciation for the year was ₹1,00,000 and a machine with a written-down value of ₹50,000 was sold. Machinery PURCHASED during the year was:

options.explanation: ₹2,00,000 forgets the depreciation charged through the account.
₹1,50,000 is only the raw balance movement, ignoring depreciation and the disposal.
Purchases = closing 9,50,000 + depreciation 1,00,000 + WDV sold 50,000 − opening 8,00,000 = ₹3,00,000.
₹2,50,000 forgets the ₹50,000 written-down value that left on the sale.

readLink.label: Read: Building the statement →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — asset account reconstruction for investing flows

questions.stem: The difference between the direct and indirect methods of preparing a cash flow statement lies in:

options.explanation: Financing and investing sections are untouched by the choice of method.

options.text: The operating section only — investing and financing are identical, and both methods give the same cash from operations
options.explanation: Direct shows gross receipts/payments; indirect un-does accruals from net profit; the destination figure is the same.
The cash-equivalent definition is common to both methods.

options.text: The total of the statement — the direct method reports higher cash
options.explanation: Both methods must arrive at the identical cash from operations.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — direct and indirect methods

questions.stem: Sales for the year were ₹20,00,000 (all on credit) and trade receivables increased by ₹1,60,000. Under the direct method, cash received from customers is:

options.explanation: ₹21,60,000 adds the receivables increase — money still stuck with customers cannot be a receipt.
Receipts = sales − increase in receivables = 20,00,000 − 1,60,000 = ₹18,40,000.
₹20,00,000 is accrual sales, not the cash actually collected.
₹16,80,000 deducts the increase twice.

readLink.label: Read: Building the statement →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — direct method receipts

questions.stem: Under AS 3, a movement in bank overdraft / cash credit during the year is presented as:

options.text: A financing activity — overdrafts are borrowings, not negative cash
options.explanation: AS 3 treats bank borrowings, including overdrafts and cash credit, as financing flows.

options.text: Part of cash and cash equivalents
options.explanation: Folding overdrafts into cash is the Ind AS/IAS 7 cash-management idea — AS 3 does not adopt it.
Nothing is being invested — the overdraft is a borrowing.
An overdraft finances the business; drawing it down is not an operating flow.

readLink.label: Read: Cash flow statements — the AS 3 frame →

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — bank borrowings as financing; SM Ch 11 U2

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Case: Narmada Shipworks (classification with a long cycle)
questions.case: Narmada Shipworks Ltd builds fishing trawlers. It holds raw material for 4 months, spends 3 months in fabrication, holds finished craft for 5 months and collects from customers in 3 months. At 31 March its balances include: inventory expected to convert to cash in 14 months; a ₹12,00,000 term loan repayable in six half-yearly instalments of ₹2,00,000 each, the first falling due 6 months after the balance sheet date; and a security deposit with the state electricity board refundable when the connection is surrendered, expected after three years.

questions.stem: The company's normal operating cycle is:

options.explanation: Twelve months is only the fallback where the cycle CANNOT be identified — here it can.
3 months is merely the collection period.
4 + 3 + 5 + 3 = 15 months from acquiring materials to realising cash.
9 months counts only some legs; the cycle runs through all four stages to cash.

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — operating cycle

questions.stem: The inventory expected to convert in 14 months is classified as:

options.text: Current — it will be realised within the 15-month operating cycle
options.explanation: The cycle test is an independent, sufficient route to current classification, even past twelve months.
The twelve-month test is not the only test; realisation within the operating cycle suffices.

options.text: Split: twelve months' worth current, the rest non-current
options.explanation: No time-apportionment exists — the whole item passes the cycle test.

options.text: Current only if management so elects
options.explanation: Classification follows the Schedule III tests, not an election.

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — current assets, operating-cycle test

questions.stem: Of the ₹12,00,000 term loan, the amount classified under current liabilities is:

options.explanation: The whole loan is not current; only the twelve-month slice moves down.
Instalments due at months 6 and 12 fall within twelve months: 2 × ₹2,00,000 = ₹4,00,000 current; ₹8,00,000 long-term.
Current maturities of long-term debt must be reclassified — two instalments qualify.
₹2,00,000 counts only the first instalment — the second, due exactly at month 12, is also within twelve months.

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — current maturities of long-term debt

questions.stem: The electricity-board security deposit is presented as:

options.text: A non-current asset (long-term loans and advances/other non-current assets)
options.explanation: Refund is expected after three years — outside both the cycle and twelve months.

options.text: A contingent asset in the notes
options.explanation: The refund right is real, not contingent — it is a recognised asset.

options.text: A current asset, since deposits are always liquid
options.explanation: Liquidity in form does not override an expected three-year holding.
A deposit refundable in three years is nowhere near a cash equivalent.

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — non-current assets

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Case: Chenab Textiles (capital and P&L disclosures)
questions.case: Chenab Textiles Ltd began the year with 8,00,000 fully paid-up equity shares of face value ₹10. During the year it made a rights issue of one share for every four held, fully subscribed and paid at par. Customer billings for the year totalled ₹62,40,000, which includes GST of ₹6,40,000 collected on behalf of government. After the year end but before the accounts were approved, the Board proposed a final dividend of ₹1.50 per share.

questions.stem: The share-reconciliation note shows closing shares outstanding of:

options.explanation: 9,00,000 halves the rights entitlement; a 1:4 issue on 8,00,000 shares adds 2,00,000.
8,00,000 ignores the rights issue the note exists to show.
12,00,000 treats the ratio as 1:2 — it was one for every FOUR.

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — reconciliation of shares outstanding

questions.stem: Revenue from operations for the year is:

options.explanation: ₹62,40,000 counts the GST as the company's own revenue — it is a collection for government.
₹49,60,000 strips the GST twice.
GST collected for government is excluded: 62,40,000 − 6,40,000 = ₹56,00,000.
₹68,80,000 adds the GST on top instead of removing it.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 11 U1 — revenue excludes taxes collected

questions.stem: In the statement of profit and loss, employee costs, finance costs and depreciation appear:

options.explanation: The format is statutory, not discretionary.

options.text: Only in the notes, not on the face
options.explanation: These are face-of-statement line items in the prescribed format.

options.text: Merged into cost of sales and administrative expenses
options.explanation: That is function-wise classification — not the Division I format.

options.text: As separate NATURE-wise line items prescribed by Division I
options.explanation: Employee benefits expense, finance costs, and depreciation & amortisation are each prescribed heads.

questions.sourceRef: Schedule III Division I — Part II

questions.stem: The dividend proposed after the year end is:

options.text: Deducted directly from surplus in reserves
options.explanation: No appropriation is made until the dividend is declared.

options.text: Disclosed in the notes; recognised as a liability only when declared
options.explanation: Under AS 4 (as amended), a post-year-end proposal is a non-adjusting event — note disclosure only.

options.text: Provided for as a current liability at the year end
options.explanation: Providing for proposed dividend is the OLD treatment; AS 4 as amended forbids it.
It cannot be ignored — the notes must disclose it.

questions.sourceRef: AS 4 (amended) — proposed dividend; SM Ch 11 U1

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Cash flow · Case: Periyar Chemicals (indirect method, all sections)
questions.case: Periyar Chemicals Ltd (a non-financial company) reports for the year: net profit before tax ₹6,50,000; depreciation ₹1,20,000; profit on sale of investments ₹20,000; interest expense ₹45,000. During the year trade receivables increased by ₹70,000, inventory decreased by ₹40,000 and trade payables increased by ₹25,000. Income tax paid was ₹1,80,000. The company purchased plant for ₹3,00,000, sold investments with a book value of ₹1,00,000 for ₹1,20,000, raised a long-term loan of ₹2,00,000, paid dividend of ₹90,000 and paid the interest of ₹45,000 in cash.

questions.stem: Operating profit before working capital changes is:

options.explanation: ₹7,50,000 leaves out the interest expense add-back of ₹45,000.
₹6,50,000 is raw net profit with no adjustments at all.
6,50,000 + depreciation 1,20,000 − profit on sale 20,000 + interest expense 45,000 = ₹7,95,000.
₹8,35,000 ADDS the ₹20,000 profit on sale — it must be deducted; the proceeds belong to investing.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — indirect method, non-cash adjustments

questions.stem: Net cash FROM OPERATING activities is:

options.explanation: ₹5,65,000 wrongly deducts the interest of ₹45,000 here — it is shown under financing.
₹6,80,000 adds the receivables increase instead of deducting it.
₹7,90,000 stops before deducting the ₹1,80,000 tax paid.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — cash generated from operations less taxes

questions.stem: Net cash flow from INVESTING activities is:

options.explanation: (₹2,00,000) uses the ₹1,00,000 book value as the inflow — the CASH received was ₹1,20,000.
−3,00,000 plant + 1,20,000 sale proceeds = −₹1,80,000. The ₹20,000 profit is already inside the proceeds.
(₹3,00,000) ignores the investment sale proceeds entirely.
(₹1,60,000) adds the ₹20,000 profit on top of the proceeds — double counting.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — investing: gross proceeds, not book values

questions.stem: Net cash flow from FINANCING activities is:

options.explanation: (₹1,35,000) drops the ₹2,00,000 loan inflow.
₹1,10,000 forgets the interest paid, a financing outflow for a non-financial company.
₹2,00,000 counts only the loan raised and no distributions.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — financing activities

questions.stem: The net INCREASE in cash and cash equivalents for the year is:

options.explanation: ₹4,30,000 omits the financing section's ₹65,000 inflow.
₹6,10,000 is only the operating section.
₹5,15,000 mis-signs the investing outflow.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — net change in cash and cash equivalents

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Cash flow · Case: Kaveri Motors (classification quickfire)
questions.case: Kaveri Motors Ltd, a vehicle manufacturer (not a financial enterprise), asks you to classify several items for its AS 3 cash flow statement: (i) interest received on surplus funds parked in bonds; (ii) income tax paid for the year (none of it attributable to any investing or financing transaction); (iii) equity shares issued to the vendor of a robotic welding line as full consideration; (iv) an increase in its cash credit utilisation with the bank.

questions.stem: The interest received on bonds is classified as:

options.explanation: Financing covers the company's OWN capital and borrowings, not returns earned on parked funds.
Cash was received — it must appear, under investing.
Operating treatment of interest belongs to financial enterprises.
For a non-financial enterprise, returns on investments — interest and dividends received — are investing flows.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — interest received, non-financial enterprise

questions.stem: The income tax paid is classified as:

options.explanation: No specific attribution to investing exists on these facts.

options.text: Apportioned across all three activities pro rata
options.explanation: AS 3 prescribes no pro-rata spreading of tax.
Taxes on income default to operating unless specifically attributable to investing or financing — here they are not.
No specific attribution to financing exists on these facts.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — taxes on income

questions.stem: The shares issued to the machinery vendor are:

options.text: Kept out of the cash flow statement and disclosed separately as a non-cash transaction
options.explanation: No cash flowed — AS 3 excludes non-cash investing/financing transactions from the statement.

options.text: Shown as a financing inflow with a matching investing outflow
options.explanation: Fabricating two equal flows misstates both sections — nothing cash-like happened.

options.text: Recognised only when the shares are later sold by the vendor
options.explanation: The vendor's later dealings are irrelevant to the company's statement.

options.text: Shown as an operating item since machinery makes the product
options.explanation: Machinery acquisition is never operating, and this one involved no cash at all.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — non-cash transactions

questions.stem: The increase in cash credit utilisation is presented as:

options.text: An operating inflow, as it funds working capital
options.explanation: The purpose of the borrowing does not change its financing character.

options.text: A financing inflow — cash credit is a borrowing under AS 3
options.explanation: AS 3 treats overdrafts/cash credit as bank borrowings within financing, not as negative cash.
Borrowing from a bank is not an investing activity.

options.text: A reduction of cash and cash equivalents
options.explanation: Netting overdrafts into cash follows IAS 7/Ind AS 7 thinking — AS 3 keeps them in financing.

questions.sourceRef: AS 3 — bank borrowings as financing

questions.stem: Define 'financial statements' under s.2(40) of the Companies Act 2013 and state the exemption it contains. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Financial statements include: balance sheet, statement of profit and loss (or income & expenditure account), cash flow statement, statement of changes in equity where applicable, and explanatory notes forming part thereof
Exemption: a One Person Company, small company and dormant company may exclude the cash flow statement
Statements must give a true and fair view and comply with Schedule III (s.129)

questions.topic: Ch 11 · Company FS · Books of account
questions.stem: State the requirements of s.128 of the Companies Act 2013 regarding books of account. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Kept at the registered office; may be kept at another place in India by Board decision with notice to the Registrar; electronic mode permitted
Must give a true and fair view of the company's state of affairs, including branches, and explain the transactions
Accrual basis and double-entry system are mandatory
Preservation: minimum eight financial years (longer if an investigation has been ordered)

questions.stem: State the Schedule III criteria for classifying an asset as current, and explain the role of the operating cycle. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Current if ANY one: expected realisation/sale/consumption in the normal operating cycle; held primarily for trading; expected realisation within twelve months of the reporting date; cash or cash equivalent unless restricted beyond twelve months
skeleton.citation: Schedule III Division I — general instructions

skeleton.point: All other assets are non-current
Operating cycle = time between acquisition of assets for processing and realisation in cash or cash equivalents; where unidentifiable, assumed twelve months
Effect: a long cycle (e.g. 15 months) makes 14-month inventory current — the cycle and twelve-month tests are alternatives

questions.stem: When must a liability be classified as current under Schedule III? Illustrate the 'unconditional right to defer' test. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Current if ANY one: settlement expected in the normal operating cycle; held primarily for trading; due within twelve months of the reporting date; no unconditional right to defer settlement beyond twelve months
Illustration: a loan repayable on demand is current even if the lender is not expected to demand; expectation of rollover ≠ unconditional right
Current maturities of long-term debt (instalments due within twelve months) move to current liabilities

questions.stem: List the principal disclosures Schedule III requires in the notes for share capital and reserves & surplus. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Share capital: authorised, issued, subscribed, paid-up; reconciliation of NUMBER of shares outstanding (opening → closing); rights/preferences/restrictions per class
skeleton.citation: Schedule III Division I — notes on share capital

skeleton.point: Shares held by holding/ultimate holding company and their subsidiaries; shareholders holding more than 5% with number of shares; shares reserved for options/contracts
Reserves & surplus itemised: capital reserve, securities premium, revaluation reserve, general reserve, surplus — with additions/deductions during the year
skeleton.citation: Schedule III Division I — reserves and surplus

skeleton.point: Debit balance of the statement of P&L shown as a negative figure under Surplus, even if the aggregate turns negative

questions.stem: Define cash equivalents under AS 3 and give two examples of items that qualify and two that fail. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Short-term, highly liquid investments readily convertible into KNOWN amounts of cash, subject to INSIGNIFICANT risk of change in value; working rule — maturity of three months or less from the date of acquisition
Qualify: treasury bills bought within three months of maturity; short-term deposits ≤ 3 months; redeemable preference shares acquired shortly before their fixed redemption date
Fail: equity shares (value risk); a six-month fixed deposit even in its final month (term tested at acquisition)

questions.stem: Describe the three classifications of cash flows under AS 3, with two examples of each. (6 marks)

skeleton.point: Operating: principal revenue-producing activities — receipts from customers, payments to suppliers and employees, taxes on income (unless attributable elsewhere)

Investing: acquisition/disposal of long-term assets and investments — purchase/sale of PPE, purchase/sale of investments, loans to third parties, interest/dividends received (non-financial enterprise)
Financing: changes in owners' capital and borrowings — share issue, buyback, raising/repaying loans, interest/dividends paid (non-financial enterprise)

questions.stem: Distinguish the direct method from the indirect method of reporting cash flows from operating activities. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Direct: major classes of GROSS cash receipts and payments reported (customers, suppliers, employees) — assembled from records or by adjusting sales/COGS for working-capital and accrual items
Indirect: starts from net profit; adjusts non-cash items (depreciation, provisions), items belonging to other activities (interest, profit/loss on disposals) and working-capital changes
Both produce identical cash from operations; investing and financing sections are unaffected by the choice

questions.stem: How are interest, dividends and taxes on income classified in a cash flow statement? Distinguish financial from other enterprises. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Financial enterprise: interest paid, interest received and dividends received are OPERATING (lending/borrowing is the revenue-producing activity); dividends PAID remain financing
Other enterprises: interest and dividends received → investing; interest and dividends paid → financing
Each such item disclosed separately in the statement
Taxes on income: operating unless specifically identifiable with a financing or investing transaction
